subject: Vital Knowledge: Vital Dawn for Wednesday April 8, 2026
body:
MORNING

Apr 08, 2026 5:19:23 AM EST

Vital Dawn for Wednesday April 8, 2026

Morning update

• Market levels – the US equity futures are spiking: S&P +177 points/267bp, Dow +1135 points/242bp, Nasdaq +851 points/350bp, and R2 K +97 points/380bp. The major Eurozone indices are surging 350-430bp , and the gains are very broad (all the major sub-groups are trading higher w/the exception of energy , with the SXEP index down ~4%, and utilities ). Areas of outperformance include autos, industrials, basic resources, tech, banks, travel, and retail . In addition to energy and utilities , other areas of underperformance include telecom, insurance, and chemicals . Stocks surged in Asia : TPX +3.32%, NKY +5.39%, Hang Seng +3.09%, HSCEI +2.61%, SHCOMP +2.69%, Shenzhen +4.35%, Taiwan TAIEX +4.61%, Korea KOSPI +6.87% ( Samsung Electronics surged 10% while SK Hynix rocketed higher by 16%), Australia ASX 200 +2.56%, and India is up ~3.7-3.8%. Treasuries are climbing (2-year yields are down 7bp while 10-year yields slide 6bp). Fed expectations are moving in a dovish direction, with the market now pricing in 14bp of rate cuts this year. In Europe , 10-year yields are tumbling (down ~23bp in France , 27bp in Italy , 17bp in Germany , 21bp in the UK , etc.). The market is pricing in 54bp worth rate hikes this year from the ECB , 32bp from the BOE , and 51bp from the BOJ . The DXY is slumping 100bp. Brent is off 13.5% to $94.45 (the price was ~$70 before the war). Gold is advancing 195bp while silver rises 560bp. Bitcoin is jumping 370bp (to ~$71.86 K).
• What’s happening this morning – the big news arrived at ~6:30pm ET Tues night when Trump formally announced a 2 -week ceasefire and Hormuz reopening deal . The market response to the development is as one would expect : stocks are spiking while Brent tumbles ~13.5% (to ~$94). Treasuries are rallying , although the move isn’t enormous (10-year yields are only down 6bp). Our full Iran thoughts , which we initially published last night, can be found at this link (they are also reprinted below). The one thing to add is that there is a lot of skepticism and cynicism about this ceasefire announcement , in part because several questions remain unanswered (the agreement apparently doesn’t apply to Lebanon, it’s not clear whether Iran will impose fees on ships traveling through Hormuz, Washington and Tehran still seem far apart on a final agreement, etc.) and while this is understandable, the response points to even more upside risk for equities in the immediate term as technical imbalances (positioning and sentiment) are forced to normalize . Other than geopolitics , and a beat-and-raise LEVI print , there weren’t any other major news items out since the Tues close.
• What we’ll be watching on Wednesday – the focus will be on Delta’s Q1 earnings report before the open ( RPM reports BMO too), a Pentagon press briefing at 8am ET (with Hegseth and Caine), the FOMC minutes at 2pm ET, and a handful of earnings reports after the close ( APLD and STZ ). According to Trump’s daily schedule , the president will participate in a meeting w/the Sec of Agriculture at 11:30am ET, he will participate in an interview at 2pm ET, and he meets with the Sec General of NATO at 3:30pm ET.
• What we’ll be watching on Thursday – the only major event on the calendar is the US PCE for Feb at 8:30am ET (the Street is modeling headline unchanged vs. Jan at +2.8% Y/Y while core cools 10bp to +3%).
• What we’ll be watching on Friday – the major events will be the US CPI for Mar at 8:30am ET (the Street is looking for a sharp acceleration in headline to +3.4% vs. +2.4% in Feb while core climbs to +2.7% vs. +2.5% in Feb) and the first meeting of US and Iranian officials in Pakistan .
• Market in a Minute (macro) – Japan’s wage numbers for Feb ran very hot (coming in at +1.9% vs. the Street +1.3%), strengthening the case for further BOJ policy tightening ( Bloomberg ); Chinese imports into the US haven’t dropped as much as the headline numbers might suggest as companies slash the value of their shipments “using tactics ranging from legal accounting tricks to outright fraud” ( NYT ); Insurance & private credit : the Treasury is looking to consult w/state insurance regulators about the massive holdings of private credit assets accumulating in insurance company investment portfolios ( WSJ ); Republican Clay Fuller won the special House election in Georgia , as expected, but only by ~12 points (down from the 37 points that Trump won the district by in 2024 ) ( NYT ); The Democratic-backed candidate in Wisconsin’s Supreme Court election won in a 20-point landslide ( Politico )
• Market in a Minute (micro – Wednesday morning) – Danone is working on a potential purchase of Reckitt Benckiser’s Mead Johnson business according to a report in La Lettre (Bloomberg); Shell said Q1 gas volumes would be negatively impacted by events in the Middle East, but oil trading should see a large boost ( WSJ )
• Market in a Minute (micro – Tuesday night) - F (Ford) has sought relief from the White House on aluminum tariffs until the Novelis facility in NY is back online following a fire, but its entreaties have been rebuffed ( WSJ ); LEVI (Levi Strauss) reported solid upside in FQ1 on EPS at 42c (vs. the Street 37c), w/the beat driven by higher sales (revenue came in +14% headline and +9% organic to $1.742 B vs. the Street $1.648 B) and better margins (op. margins of 12.5% were nearly 50bp ahead of plan), and the F26 guidance is moving higher; MGPI (MGP Ingredients) announced plans to temporarily idle two distilling operations as the company adjusts production levels to align with its current inventory levels (“The American whiskey market continues to be structurally oversupplied, with excess capacity and elevated inventory”); OWL (Blue Owl) : the outlook on Blue Owl Credit Income Corp. was cut to negative from stable by Moody’s (the rating was left unchanged at Baa2) ( Reuters ); SMCI (Supermicro) confirmed that an independent investigation is underway regarding the March 2026 indictment of three individuals who were associated with the Company at that time (the indictments related to illicit server sales to China)
• Calendar for Thursday 4/9 – the focus will be on German exports and industrial production for Feb (2am ET), earnings before the US open ( SMPL ), US personal income/spending for Feb (8:30am ET), the US PCE for Feb (8:30am ET), the US GDP revisions for Q4 (8:30am ET ), US wholesale inventories/trade sales for Feb (10am ET), earnings after the US close ( WDFC ), Japan’s PPI for Mar (Thurs night), China’s PPI/CPI for Mar (Thurs night), and the Bank of Korea policy decision (Thurs night).
• Calendar for Friday 4/10 – the focus will be on Taiwan’s imports/exports for Mar (4am ET), the US-Iran meeting in Pakistan , the US CPI for Mar (8:30am ET), US factory orders for Feb (10am ET), and the Michigan sentiment survey for Apr (10am ET).

Iran

• Trump at 6:30pm ET Tues night (about 90 minutes prior to his 8pm ET deadline) said the US would suspend its bombing campaign against Iran for a period of two weeks . This is contingent on Iran agreeing to the “complete, immediate, and safe opening” of Hormuz .
• Trump says he’s agreeing to a 14-day ceasefire because the US has “met and exceeded” all its military objectives and is “very far along” on a “definitive agreement” for “long term peace with Iran” . Trump goes on to claim that “ almost all of the various points of past contention ” between the US and Iran have been resolved, and the 2-week ceasefire will allow for an agreement “to be finalized and consummated” . The 10-point plan submitted by Iran is a “workable basis” on which to negotiate according to Trump.
• Iran has approved the 2-week proposal according to media reports , including safe passage through Hormuz , although it’s not entirely clear whether it will impose fees on ships (Tehran issued a somewhat celebratory response , claiming that it forced Washington to accept its 10-point plan ). It looks like Israel will abide by the ceasefire in Iran (although Netanyahu said it doesn’t apply to Lebanon ).
• According to Axios, the US and Iran will hold negotiations starting this Friday in Islamabad, and there is a lot of difficult negotiating to be done (on enrichment, nuclear material, missiles, reparations and/or Hormuz tolls, proxy groups, sanctions, etc.). Meanwhile, the Iran regime didn’t only survive , but it’s now embittered .
• Our view for a while has been that since Trump faced only horrible escalatory options from here (destroying civilian infrastructure, militarily reopening Hormuz, and/or seizing the enriched uranium), since Iran obtained an ability and showed a willingness to wage a campaign of economic terror throughout the region , since the global economy was already buckling under the weight of a powerful burst of stagflationary pressure from the last 5 weeks of fighting (this is still going to pass through the data and company EPS for at least the next several months), since political opposition was growing in Washington (it’s doubtful a $200 B supplemental would have passed Congress, even via reconciliation), and since the US has achieved the bulk of its strategic objectives (degrading Iran’s nuclear and missile capabilities), an offramp was the more likely outcome .
• The Iraq I scenario looks increasingly likely, which will likely require an enhanced US military presence to stay in the Middle East for years working to contain Tehran (this is much better than Iraq II , which would have meant a multi-year quagmire draining the US of both blood and money).
• A 2-week ceasefire is obviously positive news, and stocks will celebrate , but keep in mind the SPX was struggling with the 6900-7000 level before the war, so that is likely to be even stronger resistance now . The economy (and Corporate America) are going to be forced to grapple with the fallout from the last 5 weeks ( this will be a months-long process, if not longer ). The FOMC is (at best) on the sidelines for the balance of the year, and the pace of Fed asset purchases will slide materiall y after this month (while the ECB, BOE, and BOJ are all likely to tighten ).
• Stocks that benefited from the war (energy, commodity chemicals, defense contractors, etc.) will probably suffer aggressive profit taking while consumer discretionary (including housing-linked names) should see the biggest rally .
• Hormuz will never go back to the way it was before – Iran’s ability to shut the waterway will embed a risk premium in the price of all commodities flowing through it for the foreseeable future (there will be an accelerated process of constructing infrastructure such that countries, including Saudi Arabia, can bypass Hormuz , but this will take years to come online ).
• The NYT on Tuesday published a long profile article detailing Trump’s decision to launch the Iran war – the main takeaways in our view were 1) the amount of opposition that existed inside the White House to the campaign (Vance, Caine, and Rubio were all opposed to varying degrees) and 2) the degree of concern inside the Pentagon about how a conflict would deplete munition stockpiles to dangerously low levels (overall, the report suggests the White Houde doesn’t plan on sustaining a protracted campaign ).

US macro

• A fierce burst of stagflationary pressure is going to soon start showing up in economic data around the world, and it will last (at least) for many months . The Manheim Used Car Index (out Tues morning) spiked 6.2% Y/Y and 1.4% M/M (the long-term average monthly move for Mar is flat on a sequential basis). The NY Fed’s consumer survey (which can be found here ) showed near-term (1-year) inflation expectations up 40bp M/M to +3.4% ( gas price expectations surged 530bp to +9.4%, the highest since Mar 2022). The US CPI for Mar on Fri is expected to show a massive acceleration (with headline going from +2.4% in Feb to +3.4% in Mar).
• Medium and long-term US inflation expectations were muted in Mar despite the Iran war according to the Fed’s latest consumer survey (available here ).
• Chinese imports into the US haven’t dropped as much as the headline numbers might suggest as companies slash the value of their shipments “using tactics ranging from legal accounting tricks to outright fraud” ( NYT )

Washington

• Republican Clay Fuller won the special House election in Georgia , as expected, but only by ~12 points (down from the 37 points that Trump won the district by in 2024 ) ( NYT )
• The Democratic-backed candidate in Wisconsin’s Supreme Court election won in a 20-point landslide ( Politico )
• Army Sec Dan Driscoll said he has no plans to resign or otherwise depart the Pentagon ( Washington Post )

International macro

• Germany’s factory orders for Feb fell a bit short of expectations at +0.9% M/M (vs. the Street +3%) (Bloomberg)
• India’s central bank left rates unchanged, as was widely anticipated ( WSJ )
• Japan’s wage numbers for Feb ran very hot (coming in at +1.9% vs. the Street +1.3%), strengthening the case for further BOJ policy tightening ( Bloomberg )
• Taiwan’s headline CPI came in cooler than anticipated for Mar (+1.2% vs. the Street +1.6%), but core overshot the Street at +1.94% (vs. the Street +1.85%) (Bloomberg)

Consumer

• Danone is working on a potential purchase of Reckitt Benckiser’s Mead Johnson business according to a report in La Lettre (Bloomberg)
• HTZ (Hertz)/CAR (Avis) shares jumped on Tues following a spike in the Manheim used car index for March .
• LEVI (Levi Strauss) reported solid upside in FQ1 on EPS at 42c (vs. the Street 37c), w/the beat driven by higher sales (revenue came in +14% headline and +9% organic to $1.742 B vs. the Street $1.648 B) and better margins (op. margins of 12.5% were nearly 50bp ahead of plan) as mgmt. called out strength “across channels, regions, and categories” ( DTC was +10% organic, wholesale +8%, Asia +12%, Europe +10%, and Beyond Yoga +23%; the US was an area of relative weakness at +4%), and the F26 guidance is moving higher (they now see organic revenue +5% vs. the prior +4.5% w/ op. margins of 12% vs. the prior 11.9%). Despite the beat-and-raise, mgmt. acknowledges uncertainty in the environment (“the current domestic and international political environment, including volatile trade relations and military and civil conflicts, and most recently heightened military action in the Middle East, have resulted in uncertainty surrounding the future state of the global economy”). In addition to earnings, the company also announced a CFO transition .
• MGPI (MGP Ingredients) announced plans to temporarily idle two distilling operations as the company adjusts production levels to align with its current inventory levels (“ The American whiskey market continues to be structurally oversupplied , with excess capacity and elevated inventory”) but despite this, the prior 2026 guidance is being reaffirmed .
• Remy Cointreau announced an “ ambitious transformation plan ” as the company looks to “ regain momentum ” in its business.

Energy/Industrials

• Airlines + fuel: the head of the IATA warns it could take months for jet fuel supplies to recover even if Hormuz is fully reopened ( Reuters )
• F (Ford) has sought relief from the White House on aluminum tariffs until the Novelis facility in NY is back online following a fire, but its entreaties have been rebuffed ( WSJ )
• Shell said Q1 gas volumes would be negatively impacted by events in the Middle East, but oil trading should see a large boost ( WSJ )
• Trump recently heralded a massive refinery project in Texas he claimed was worth $300 B, but the initial investment is only $40 M ( FT )
• US auto makers accuse Europe of keeping large American pick-up trucks off Eurozone roads ( FT )

Financials

• Bitcoin: a NYT profile article says the creator of bitcoin is Adam Back ( NYT )
• Insurance & private credit : the Treasury is looking to consult w/state insurance regulators about the massive holdings of private credit assets accumulating in insurance company investment portfolios ( WSJ )
• OWL (Blue Owl) : the outlook on Blue Owl Credit Income Corp. was cut to negative from stable by Moody’s (the rating was left unchanged at Baa2) ( Reuters )

M&A/Strategic Actions

• BMC Helix could be sold by KKR to Montagu in a deal worth ~$900 MM ( Bloomberg )
• Byte Dance valuation rises to more than $600 B following an equity sale by one of the founding shareholders ( SCMP )
• Danone is working on a potential purchase of Reckitt Benckiser’s Mead Johnson business according to a report in La Lettre (Bloomberg)
• TSLA (Tesla) – there is rising speculation that Musk could eventually merge Space X with Tesla ( WSJ )

TMT

• AAPL (Apple) remains on track to launch a foldable i Phone later this year despite reports of manufacturing hiccups ( Bloomberg )
• AI update : a recent NYT article talked about “ code overload” becoming a problem as AI tools generate a tsunami of code (too much for engineers to handle) while The Information wrote about the “tokenmaxxing” trend in Silicon Valley (whereby individuals compete to see how much AI tokens they can generate) – both reports raise red flags about how much AI usage is actually productive vs. experimental and/or frivolous .
• APP (App Lovin) announced succession plans relating to its executive management team and the appointment of Craig Billings as independent Chairperson of its Board of Directors .
• Byte Dance valuation rises to more than $600 B following an equity sale by one of the founding shareholders ( SCMP )
• Cybersecurity stocks rallied on Tues after Anthropic launched “Project Glasswing” , a partnership with firms such as Crowd Strike and Palo Alto Networks to help ensure cybersecurity technology can handle powerful new AI models ( Anthropic )
• INTC (Intel) shares jumped on Tues after the company said it will work on the Space X/Tesla Terafab project .
• NTNX (Nutanix) boosted its buyback authorization by $29 MM to $779 MM.
• ORCL (Oracle) – PIMCO could help provide ~$14 B of debt financing for a massive Oracle data center in Michigan ( Bloomberg )
• Perplexity has seen revenue spike as it pivots away from AI search toward agents ( FT )
• SK Hynix is in talks on long-term memory deals w/ Google and Microsoft ( Digitimes )
• SMCI (Supermicro) confirmed that an independent investigation is underway regarding the March 2026 indictment of three individuals who were associated with the Company at that time (the indictments related to illicit server sales to China). The independent directors have not set a definitive timetable for the investigation. The Company will provide an update when the investigation is complete and does not intend to comment further until that time.
• Social media restrictions: Greece will ban social media access for children under 15 ( Bloomberg )
• TSLA (Tesla) shares saw further pressure on Tues due to ongoing fallout from the soft Q1 delivery numbers along w/ doubts about Robotaxi and Optimus , a weak cash flow outlook (the Street is modeling FCF of -$5 B this year), and the Space X IPO (Space X will be carving out a huge chunk of the deal for retail investors, and some of those people could be freeing up room by selling Tesla so they can pivot into Musk’s rocket company).
• TSLA (Tesla) – there is rising speculation that Musk could eventually merge Space X with Tesla ( WSJ )
• TTD (Trade Desk) shares slumped during the Tues session on a report that three top executives will be departing the company ( Ad Week )

Calendar for the week of Monday April 13

• What to watch during the week of Monday 4/13 – the main macro events this week include China’s imports/exports for Mar (Mon night), US import/export prices for Mar (Wed morning), the Fed’s Beige Book (Wed afternoon), China’s retail sales/industrial production for Mar (Wed night), and Kevin Warsh’s confirmation hearing before the Senate Banking Committee (Thurs). For US earnings , the key reports include: Mon morning ( GS ); Tues morning ( BLK, C, JNJ, JPM, KMX , and WFC ); Wed morning ( BAC, PNC , and MS ); Wed night ( SLG ); Thurs morning ( ABT, BK, CFG, KEY, MRSH, PEP, SCHW, TRV , and USB ); Thurs night ( AA and NFLX ); and Fri morning ( RF, STT , and TFC ). In Europe, the key earnings reports include: Tues night ( Kering ); Wed morning ( ASML and Hermes ); Thurs morning ( Entain and Pernod Ricard ); and Fri morning ( Ericsson ).
• Calendar for Monday 4/13 – the focus will be on India’s CPI for Mar (6:30am ET), earnings before the US open ( FAST, GS ), US existing home sales for Mar (10am ET), and China’s imports/exports for Mar (Mon night).
• Calendar for Tuesday 4/14 – the focus will be on Japan’s industrial production for Feb (12:30am ET), earnings before the US open ( BLK, C, JNJ, JPM, KMX, WFC ), the US PPI for Mar (8:30am ET), earnings after the EU close ( Kering ), and South Korea’s import/export prices and unemployment rate for Mar (Tues night).
• Calendar for Wednesday 4/15 – the focus will be on earnings before the EU open ( Antofagasta, ASML, Hermes ), EU industrial production for Feb (5am ET), earnings before the US open ( BAC, PGR, PNC, MS, MTB ), the US Empire Manufacturing Index for Apr (8:30am ET), US import/export prices for Mar (8:30am ET), the NAHB housing market index for Apr (10am ET), the Fed’s Beige Book (2pm ET), earnings after the US close ( HOMB, SLG ), China’s new home prices for Mar (Wed night), Australia’s jobs report for Mar (Wed night), and China’s retail sales/industrial production for Mar and GDP for Q1 (Wed night).
• Calendar for Thursday 4/16 – the focus will be on earnings before the EU open ( Entain, Pernod Ricard, Rentokil, Tesco earnings ), UK GDP and industrial/manufacturing production for Feb (2am ET), earnings before the US open ( ABT, BK, CFG, KEY, MRSH, PEP, PLD, SCHW, TRV, USB ), US weekly jobless claims (8:30am ET), the Philadelphia Fed index for April (8:30am ET), US industrial/manufacturing production for March (9:15am ET), and earnings after the US close ( AA, NFLX ).
• Calendar for Friday 4/17 – the focus will be on earnings before the EU open ( Ericsson ), the Eurozone trade balance for Feb (5am ET), and earnings before the US open ( ALLY, ALV, FITB, RF, STT, TFC ).

The fight against AI data centers escalates...

April 08, 2026

Presented By

Rise and grind. Unhappy with the NBA’s dunk contest, Shaq is launching his own professional dunk league . It sounds cool, but if you wanted to see people get dunked on mercilessly, you could always just ask your mom if she’s seen anyone you went to high school with recently.
—Dave Lozo, Sam Klebanov, Matty Merritt, Adam Epstein
In today’s newsletter, we’ll look at:

• Growing backlash to data centers
• President Trump agreeing to a two-week Iran ceasefire
• The shrinking US middle class

*Stock data as of market close, cryptocurrency data as of 4:00pm ET. Here's what these numbers mean.

• Markets: Stocks initially sank yesterday in response to President Trump’s apocalyptic threat to Iran (more on that later), but they recovered late in the day on hopes of a last-minute deal. Meanwhile, Apple fell following a report that the release of its foldable i Phone could get delayed.

CENTER OF ATTENTION

The fight against data centers is escalating

Jim West/Getty Images

Yesterday, 13 bullets were fired at the home of an Indianapolis city councilor who recently supported the construction of a data center in his district, the latest chapter in the growing backlash against the controversial buildings that power AI.
The elected official, Ron Gibson, said that a note left at the scene (where no one was physically harmed) read “No Data Centers.” The attack came days after he voted in favor of a data center project in an Indianapolis neighborhood where many locals had fought against it.
Maine and other states are pushing back
The state known for lobster and “oh, you mean that Portland” is expected to pass legislation this spring that would place a moratorium on data centers that consume more than 20 megawatts—a rebuke of the swelling energy costs that come with these computing factories. Other states are considering similar measures:

• Statewide bans are on the table in nine states; Pennsylvania would make 10 if it proposes one of its own, which is expected.
• Activists in Ohio are collecting signatures to get a statewide ban on the ballot in November.
• Two states—South Dakota and Wisconsin—have rejected proposed bans on new data centers. And that’s not all. Residents in Port Washington, WI, voted yesterday on a measure that would stop future data center development in the town of ~12,000 people. There are at least three other municipalities around the country that will consider something similar this year.
Big picture: While evidence shows that data centers can be environmental hazards (a new study says that they create heat islands within a 6-mile radius that can cause pollution and deaths), they are also job creators. The response to the bill in Maine, where a Senate seat is up for grabs in November, could serve as a “ canary in the coal mine ” for officials in other locales, according to a construction trade group that spoke to the Wall Street Journal. —DL

Sponsored By Pendulum

Au naturel GLP-1 support

Did you hear about the group of Ph D scientists from Johns Hopkins, Harvard, and Stanford who walked into a lab—and left with a breakthrough probiotic in hand?
These scientists zeroed in on one of the most beneficial strains for gut health, Akkermansia muciniphila . They figured out how to bring live Akkermansia muciniphila to the probiotics market. The final result of their work? Pendulum’s Akkermansia probiotic .
This particular strain is so groundbreaking because it can strengthen gut lining, improve metabolic and digestive health , and naturally increase GLP-1.*
With more than 16k medical provider recommendations, 4.5k mentions of Akkermansia muciniphila in scientific publications, and loyal customers like Halle Berry, this stuff sure sounds promising.
Try Akkermansia yourself and take up to 62% off .

WORLD

Tour de headlines

Fatemeh Bahrami/Getty Images

Trump agrees to two-week ceasefire with Iran, subject to Strait of Hormuz opening. In a post on Truth Social, President Trump said he had agreed to a proposal by Pakistan to stop bombing Iran for two weeks while diplomatic negotiations continue, subject to Iran reopening the Strait of Hormuz. Israel also agreed to the ceasefire, CNN reported. Trump’s post came roughly 90 minutes before his 8pm deadline for Iran to reopen the strait or else “a whole civilization will die,” which the president had threatened in an earlier post. Trump’s threat was condemned by US politicians across the aisle, as well as world leaders and Pope Leo XIV. Earlier on Tuesday, the US struck targets on Iran’s Kharg Island, the country’s main oil hub.
Bill Ackman’s fund wants to buy Universal Music Group. The hedge fund billionaire best known for writing really long tweets could soon own the music company behind Taylor Swift and Bad Bunny. Ackman’s Pershing Square is offering to buy Universal Music Group (UMG) for about $64.4 billion , according to a statement. Ackman praised the company for building a “world-class artist roster,” but argued that its stock price has “languished due to a combination of issues that are unrelated to the performance of its music business.” If the deal goes through, UMG, which currently lists on the Euronext Amsterdam, would merge with Pershing Square and list on the New York Stock Exchange instead.
Music festival canceled after Kanye West banned from entering UK. Don’t bother with a late registration to this year’s Wireless Festival in London, because it’s not happening now that would-be headliner Ye, formerly known as Kanye West, was barred from entering the UK over his history of antisemitism. Following widespread backlash to Ye’s role at the festival, the UK government rescinded his authorization to travel to the country on the basis that it would not benefit the public good, The Guardian reported. Ye, who has publicly praised Hitler and denied the Holocaust, apologized for his remarks earlier this year, saying they were due to his bipolar disorder. —AE

Upper middle class is US’ biggest income group

Niv Bavarsky

The ranks of Americans that can add guac to their burrito bowl guilt-free have swelled. The upper middle class has become the largest income group in the US, according to a recent analysis by the right-leaning American Enterprise Institute, highlighted by the Wall Street Journal.
The report contends that the lower rungs of the middle class shrank because more Americans got richer:

• In 2024, 31% of American families were upper middle class, compared with just 10% in 1979.
• The think tank’s report defined the upper middle class as a family of three earning $133k to $400k in 2024 dollars—five to 15 times the federal poverty line. Meanwhile, the share of American households living in or near poverty declined from almost 30% in 1979 to below 19% in 2024.
‘We’re comfortable’ class
The Wall Street Journal notes that upper middle class folks are often white-collar professionals in dual-income households, who have benefitted from women’s advances in the workforce and rising wages in fields like software engineering. Many are boomers raking in sizable pension payments made possible by stock market gains.
This group’s shopping habits are behind the rise of bougie goods and services, like first-class plane tickets, which have been a major driver of US economic growth.
But… economic inequality has also grown, with families in higher tax brackets seeing greater income growth while the poorest 5% seeing their earnings decline. —SK

Founders: Get ready, set, and sorted. No need to scramble when investors call. The Fundraise-Ready Startup Kit has templates for cap tables, pitch decks, and a due diligence checklist before the pressure hits. No fuss, no panic—just clean materials that help you prepare. Confidence attracts capital. Grab the kit .

WITH A “B”

The billion-dollar health startup with a lot of red flags

MEDVi

The AI-powered telehealth startup Medvi was the subject of a New York Times profile that touted the company’s expected $1.8 billion in sales this year and its rapid growth with just 2 employees. But critics have since claimed that the company has as many red flags as a Forbes 30 under 30 recipient.
Medvi’s founder Matthew Gallagher told the NYT that he started the company, which offers GLP-1 weight loss drugs, with just $20,000 and AI software—vibe-coding its entire website, deploying AI customer service agents, and using Midjourney to make content for ads.
It sounds like the real-life version of Open AI CEO Sam Altman’s 2024 dream of an AI-powered, one-person company worth $1 billion…but other news outlets report:

• Fake before-and-after patient images appeared on Medvi’s site as recently as last month, according to Futurism.
• Medvi uses affiliate marketing that seems to rely on fake doctors and AI-generated testimonials for its Facebook ads , according to Business Insider. The company responded, “If we find an affiliate doing this we work to take these ads down.” And more officially… the company is facing two lawsuits accusing it of violating spam laws with unsolicited texts and emails (which Medvi denies). Plus, the FDA sent Medvi a warning letter over misleading claims about some of its weight loss products, which the company told BI were made by an affiliate marketer on a website that’s since been taken down.— MM

STAT

Prime number: Accuracy of Google’s AI Overviews

Morning Brew Design

When you Google “do dogs know math?” and other important questions as you lie in bed at night, Google’s AI Overviews will provide an accurate response about 90% of the time, according to new research from the AI startup Oumi. While that seems pretty good, there are caveats:

• Since Google processes trillions of searches per year, 90% accuracy still equates to tens of millions of inaccuracies per hour (or hundreds of thousands per minute), the New York Times noted.
• More than half of accurate responses linked to sources that did not actually support the information.
• The second- and fourth-most-cited sources were...Facebook and Reddit. Google concedes that AI Overviews aren’t always accurate and encourages users to double check the info. And, for the record, dogs do possess a rudimentary understanding of math (I learned that from Google’s AI Overview). —AE

**Good morning investors! **Stocks bounced back yesterday due to hope of a cease fire or break in attacks.

———————————————————————————

📊 **Economy and News**** **

### Oil Hits a New High as Attacks Delayed for Two Weeks

Real-world physical oil prices surged to a record high on Tuesday, signaling acute stress in the global energy market as tensions escalate with Iran.

Dated Brent, which tracks actual physical cargoes of North Sea crude loaded onto tankers, [hit ]( per barrel**]( ]( surpassing levels seen during Russia’s 2022 invasion of Ukraine and the 2008 financial crisis.

This contrasts sharply with Brent futures (June contract), [which were trading around ](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c20qv0w1j1do)[**$110 per barrel**](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c20qv0w1j1do), well below the 2008 peak of $147. The wide gap between physical and futures prices reflects growing concerns over immediate supply shortages rather than expectations of a quick resolution.

According to Société Générale strategists, early-March inventory buffers are thinning rapidly, leaving the system with little cushion against further disruptions. Before the conflict, the market faced a large surplus, but stockpiles have been drawn down sharply in recent weeks.

However, there may be temporary relief as Trump has[ agreed to suspending Iran attacks for two weeks]( We may also see prices fall in this period.

The move was “subject to the Islamic Republic of Iran agreeing to the COMPLETE, IMMEDIATE, and SAFE OPENING of the Strait of Hormuz,” Trump [wrote on Truth Social.](https://truthsocial.com/@real Donald Trump/posts/116365796713313030)

The announcement came less than two hours before his imposed deadline on Iran to either make a deal that includes opening the Strait of Hormuz or face major attacks on its civilian infrastructure.

Trump wrote on Truth Social he made the decision “based on conversations with Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif and Field Marshal Asim Munir, of Pakistan.”

“This will be a double sided CEASEFIRE!” Trump declared.

Stocks rallied after the news.

### **Global hits:**
• US short-term credit market[ shows early signs of stress ]( Iran war persists.
• Japan’s economy index[ falls 1.6 points in February](https://jen.jiji.com/jc/i?g=eco&k=2026040700918).
• Williams [expects 2026 inflation at 2.75%]( cites war impact.

**Reminder**: UBS[ lowers 2026 S&P 500 target](https://www.thestreet.com/investing/ubs-resets-2026-sp-500-target) on Middle East conflict risks.

### **Levi Strauss Beats Expectations and Raises Guidance**

Levi Strauss & Co.[ reported stronger-than-expected first-quarter results]( beating Wall Street estimates on both revenue and earnings, and raised its full-year guidance.

The denim giant posted solid growth, though roughly half of it came from higher prices rather than increased volume.

While the new full-year outlook is slightly below analysts’ expectations on earnings, it does not yet factor in recent tariff rollbacks, which could provide an additional boost later in the year.

Levi now expects full-year adjusted EPS of $1.42 to $1.48 and sales growth of 5.5% to 6.5%.

**Interesting**: Delta Air Lines and Southwest Airlines joined United and Jet Blue[ in raising checked bag fees ]( prices going up by 10$.

**Surprising**: Apple shares sunk 2% after reports that the[ company’s foldable i Phones may face delays]( Apple competitors Samsung and Huawei have had foldable phones since 2019. In other news, Elon Musk [is seeking to have Open AI CEO Sam Altman and President Greg Brockman removed]( from their roles as officers in the company as part of a case expected to go to trial later this month.

**Chip news**: ASML shares fell after [U.S. lawmakers proposed a law]( that would add further semiconductor export curbs on China. The restrictions would target ASML’s deep ultraviolet (DUV) lithography machine, which Chinese companies have so far been able to access.

Elsewhere, Broadcom jumped after announcing [plans to expand an agreement]( with Google for producing AI chips.

Activist investor Bill Ackman’s Pershing Square [has proposed a $64 billion takeover of Universal Music Group (UMG)]( at 30.4 euros per share — a 78% premium to the April 2 closing price.

The cash-and-stock deal includes a board refresh with Michael Ovitz as chairman and a new compensation package for CEO Lucian Grainge.

UMG shares jumped 11% on the news Tuesday. The transaction would move UMG’s primary listing to the New York Stock Exchange and is expected to close by the end of 2026.

———————————————————————————

💵 **Personal Finance**

### **Sudden Riches Syndrome: How Lottery Winners and Heirs Lose It All (And How You Won’t)**

Roughly 70% of lottery winners go bankrupt within seven years. Professional athletes and inheritance recipients fare little better. Sudden wealth triggers the same psychological response as trauma: the brain treats new money as temporary, leading to reckless spending and isolation.

The first 30 days are critical. Do nothing irreversible. Park the money in a high-yield savings account and tell exactly zero people outside your immediate family. Celebrity money manager Peter Guber calls this the “cone of silence” period. Assemble a team: fee-only fiduciary advisor, tax attorney, and estate planner. Pay them hourly or flat fees—never commissions.

Create a “wealth preservation trust” before touching a dime. This legal structure protects assets from lawsuits, divorces, and poor decisions. Fund it gradually over 24 months to avoid emotional whiplash. Behavioral economists call this “pre-commitment”—removing future temptation.

Lifestyle inflation destroys more fortunes than bad investments. The golden rule: never increase your fixed expenses by more than 10% of new income in year one. Keep the same car, same house, same friends. One NFL player famously kept his college apartment for three years after signing a $40 million contract.

Philanthropy and family gifting need guardrails. Set up donor-advised funds or family limited partnerships with clear rules. Require adult children to match any gift with their own earned income. Studies show unearned money given without skin in the game rarely creates productive adults.

Investing follows the same boring rules that built the wealth: low-cost index funds, real estate with positive cash flow, and alternative assets no larger than 10% of the portfolio. Sudden wealth syndrome victims chase “opportunities” from friends and family. The correct answer is almost always “no.”

Document everything. Write a personal money manifesto while the emotions are fresh. Revisit it annually. Sudden money is a test of character, not intellect. Pass the test by slowing down, professionalizing, and remembering the person you were before the windfall. The money can last generations—or disappear in months. The difference is almost never luck. It’s systems.

subject: Big relief rally, for now
body:
What matters in U.S. and global markets today

Morning Bid U.S.

Morning Bid U.S.

A Reuters Open Interest newsletter

What matters in U.S. and global markets today

By Alun John , Breaking News Correspondent, Finance and Markets

Markets exhaled on Wednesday after news of a two-week ceasefire between the U.S. and Iran, first announced by U.S. President Donald Trump on Tuesday evening, shortly before his deadline for Tehran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

Oil prices dropped below $100 a barrel on the announcement, while stocks and bond prices spiked and the dollar shed some of its recent safe-haven allure. But traders and analysts will be wary about the staying power of the ceasefire and the prospects for a long-lasting resolution to the war and energy shock.

I’ll get into that and more below.

But first, listen to the latest episode of the Morning Bid daily podcast . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

Data refreshes every time you open this email. For more U.S. market news, click here . Please send any feedback to morningbid@thomsonreuters.com .

Today's Market Minute

•

President Trump agreed to a two-week ceasefire with Iran ahead of his deadline for Tehran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz on Tuesday, and Iranian foreign minister Abbas Araqchi said safe passage through the waterway will be possible if attacks against Iran stop .

• Oil fell below $100 per barrel ‌on Wednesday after President Trump announced the two-week ceasefire, with Brent and WTI futures sliding around 14% and 15%, respectively .

• Shares of South Korean chipmaker SK Hynix surged on Wednesday ‌as peer Samsung Electronics’ market-beating quarterly earnings forecast a day earlier fuelled expectations about SK Hynix's results .

• Hopes that the U.S.-Iran ceasefire will quickly restore normal oil and gas flows from the Middle East are likely misplaced, argues ROI Energy Columnist Ron Bousso .
• Physical oil markets are likely to remain stressed despite the planned U.S.-Iran ceasefire, argues ROI Asia Commodities Columnist Clyde Russell .

Big relief rally, for now

Pretty much every global asset has been focused on the oil price since the start of the Iran war, so it's not surprising that with news of the ceasefire sending both Brent and WTI futures back well below $100 a barrel, we have seen some big moves around the world.

In equities, S&P 500 futures are up 2.5% , while major indexes in Europe and Asia have surged , posting or set to post their biggest daily gains since April last year. Japan’s Nikkei gained over 5% and South Korea’s KOSPI more than 6%, while Europe's broad STOXX 600 was up by around 3.5% in early trading.

It's the key beneficiaries of cheaper energy and falling yields that are leading the charge - travel, banks, tech and industrials - and a South Korean standout was chipmaker SK Hynix’s 15% rise , helped by high expectations for its own results after peer Samsung’s monster quarterly profit forecast on Tuesday. Energy stocks are the biggest fallers on the day, but they're still well up on the year.

The safe-haven greenback slipped, meanwhile. The dollar index fell from recent 11-month highs as the U.S. currency retreated on all developed market peers - even the under-fire Japanese yen, which retreated from the 160-per-dollar level after nearly crossing that threshold on Tuesday.

Bond prices have surged on the announcement , with U.S. Treasury yields falling as traders brought the prospect of Fed rate cuts back to the table. Euro zone government bond yields also fell, with traders scaling back bets for rate hikes from the ECB. But the biggest moves have been in Britain, where yields are down over 20 basis points.

Traders will be hoping that the two-week ceasefire is effective and lasts as intended. It's conditional, of course, on Tehran reopening the Strait of Hormuz, which it said it's prepared to do if attacks on Iran cease.

Markets certainly aren't telling us we're out of the woods yet. Brent above $90 would have been seen as a bad thing for the world economy and stocks just a couple of months ago, and we aren't anywhere close to the amount of central bank easing priced just before the war.

So, to keep the optimism up, U.S.-Iran negotiations - slated to begin on Friday - will need to lead somewhere brighter than before, with a number of thorny issues likely to prove contentious.

Still, news of the ceasefire marks a night-and-day difference compared to President Trump’s increasingly bellicose rhetoric and threats earlier in the week, and the relief rally reflects that.

Today's key chart

Graphics are produced by Reuters.

A temporary halt in fighting and the reopening of Hormuz would allow Middle Eastern exporters to ship significant volumes of oil that have been trapped inside the Gulf since hostilities began, offering global energy markets some ⁠immediate relief, writes ROI Energy Columnist Ron Bousso .

Today's events to watch

• U.S. 10-year note auction (1 p.m. EDT)

subject: Two-week ceasefire in Iran
body:
Plus, Trump tariffs one year on.

Daily Briefing

Daily Briefing

By Anisha De

Hello. US and Iran agree to a two-week ceasefire with the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, a Trump‑backed Republican wins the Georgia runoff , and here's the math behind the Space X IPO valuation .

Plus, Trump claimed tariffs would benefit the American economy in a big way. Can you draw what really happened ?

Today's Top News

A man shouts slogans after a two-week ceasefire in the Iran war was announced, in Tehran, Iran, April 8, 2026. Majid Asgaripour/WANA (West Asia News Agency) via REUTERS

War in the Middle East

•

The United States and Iran have agreed to a two-week ceasefire , reaching a deal less than two hours before President Donald Trump's deadline for Tehran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. White House reporter Steve Holland joins today's Reuters World News podcast to explain why the terms of the ceasefire still aren't clear, and how negotiations between the two sides for a longer-term agreement could take shape later in the week.

• The FBI warned US state and local law enforcement of an elevated threat posed by Iran's government to targets in the United States last month even as ‌the White House sought to downplay the likelihood of an attack, a law enforcement intelligence report reviewed by Reuters shows.
•

Gulf states including Saudi ‌Arabia and the UAE are exploring a $2,500 Ukrainian-designed interceptor drone as a cheaper way to counter Iranian attacks that are depleting stockpiles of US-made missiles, a Japanese firm marketing the technology overseas told Reuters.
•

The Iran-aligned Iraqi armed group Kataib Hezbollah has released abducted US journalist Shelly Kittleson , US Secretary of State Marco Rubio said.

In other news

•

Republican Clay Fuller, a former prosecutor endorsed by Trump, won a runoff election in Georgi a to replace conservative firebrand Marjorie Taylor Greene in the US House of Representatives, according to media reports.

• Nigel Farage's Reform UK said it was proposing to deny visas to people from countries seeking amends for transatlantic slavery , a move swiftly condemned by the Caribbean reparations commission as a "legacy of ‌toxic racism".
•

Greece will ban access to social media for children under the age ‌of 15 from January 1, 2027, Prime Minister Kyriakos Mitsotakis said, citing rising anxiety, sleep problems and the addictive design of online platforms.
•

Taiwan opposition leader Cheng Li-wun pledged to channel the spirit of her party's founder Sun Yat-sen and seek reconciliation with China , offering praise at his tomb for the country's achievements ‌following the communist revolution.

Business & Markets

Teenagers pose for a picture with a phone displaying the Tik Tok logo, in Bonn, Germany, February 20, 2026. REUTERS/Jana Rodenbusch/File Photo

•

Tik Tok plans to invest 1 ‌billion euros to build a second data centre in Finland in less than a year as it moves data storage for European users to the continent, company officials said. Read our exclusive report .
•

Elon Musk’s Space X is seeking a $1.75 trillion valuation in its forthcoming initial public offering. That would immediately make the company the sixth-largest US-listed firm. But, how does the math work on such a massive valuation?
•

Intel said it will join Musk's Terafab AI chip complex project with Space X and Tesla to make processors powering the tech billionaire's robotics and data center ambitions.
•

Anthropic announced an initiative called Claude Mythos Preview with major technology companies, including Amazon.com, Microsoft and Apple, that lets partners preview an advanced model with cybersecurity capabilitie s developed by the AI startup.

• The US vaping market, long dominated by Chinese imports, is seeing an uptick of "Made in America" products , in what some analysts and industry executives say is a reaction to the Trump administration's crackdown on unlicensed brands.
•

A US-imposed oil blockade has choked off Cuba’s supplies, which is the effective lifeblood of the country. Cuba’s dependence on oil is not unique in the Caribbean, but the scale of that dependence is. Read why Russian oil only delays Cuba’s petroleum problem .

Trump’s tariff talking points: One year on

Donald Trump delivers remarks on tariffs in the Rose Garden at the White House in Washington, D.C., US April 2, 2025. REUTERS/Carlos Barria/File Photo

On April 2 of last year, a little over two months into his second term, President Donald Trump unveiled sweeping tariffs on imports from almost all countries.
In his remarks that day, dubbed as “Liberation Day,” Trump made broad claims about how tariffs would reap economic benefits for America, including increasing growth, reducing the trade deficit and creating manufacturing jobs.

Since then, the US tariffs on many impacted countries have been renegotiated, caveated and even revoked. In February, the US Supreme Court ruled that Trump didn’t have the constitutional authority to impose universal tariffs, immediately invalidating them. For more on trade and tariffs, sign up for the Reuters Tariff Watch newsletter.
Can you guess the economic impact of Trump's tariffs? Take our quiz and see how your predictions stack up.

Take the test

And Finally...

Giuliana Gorini, 93, a resident at "Aris Senior Living" is served a dish of pasta during lunch at the facility in Rome, Italy, March 19, 2026. REUTERS/Guglielmo Mangiapane/File Photo

"Miss home? Why would I? I came here to live better. There is always someone around and I am always in company," said 79-year-old Bruna Bruni, a guest at Aris Senior Living.
As Italians live longer and traditional family care weakens, growing numbers of sprightly seniors are tempted by new residential developments - known as "silver housing" - that promise independence with community life and services.

Dear Investor,

On December 29, 1940 — almost a full year before the U.S.
officially entered World War II — President Roosevelt delivered
one of the most important speeches in American history.

Standing before a radio microphone in the White House, he warned
the nation that the world was changing fast... and that America
had a responsibility unlike any other country on Earth.

America, he said, must become the "Arsenal of Democracy."

Factories would have to surge into production… Shipyards would
have to build fleets of vessels… Assembly lines would have to
produce tanks, aircraft, and ammunition at unprecedented speed…

Roosevelt understood that wars aren't won by weapons alone;
they're won with the materials that make those weapons possible.

One of those materials was a little-known metal called antimony.

Without it, the lead in bullets is too soft... rounds deform...
weapons jam... and ammunition becomes unreliable.

In other words, without antimony... the bullets American
soldiers fired in World War II simply wouldn't work the way they
were supposed to.

So the U.S. government acted quickly. One remote mine in Idaho —
called Stibnite — was pushed into round-the-clock production. By
the end of the war, it supplied roughly 90% of America's
antimony.

But after the war, something predictable happened. America shut
down its mines, because foreign supply was cheaper... and for
decades, the U.S. imported the metal it once fought to produce.

Fast forward to today...

China is now the world's leading producer of antimony,
accounting for 48% of global production and 63% of U.S. antimony
imports. Russia and Tajikistan supply much of the rest — this
terrible trio altogether controls about 90% of global supply.

Meanwhile, the U.S. produces virtually none.

Which means the country that fields the most powerful military
on Earth is almost entirely dependent on foreign rivals for a
metal embedded in ammunition... electronics... batteries... and
even the protective gear on U.S. soldiers' backs.

But that's quickly changing.

Trump understands something that Roosevelt understood eighty
years ago: industrial might, and the materials that power it, are
inseparable from national security.

That's why Washington is now funneling billions of dollars into
rebuilding domestic supply chains for strategic minerals.

The U.S. Department of War just awarded a $245 million Pentagon
contract to United States Antimony Corporation to supply antimony
for the defense stockpile.

The result was swift — US Antimony's stock soared 916% in just
14 weeks.

US Antimony Stock Chart That's why I've been
investigating a small early-stage mineral exploration company
operating deep in the Nevada desert.

This small company
( )
has made a remarkable discovery in what's called the "Bullet
Zone" — that experts say could be the highest-grade discovery of
antimony in the U.S. in decades.

An astounding 15 to 85x higher grade than its closest U.S. peer.

What's more, this company
( )
has a portfolio of no less than four gold, antimony, and copper
projects located in Nevada and Idaho — two of the best mining
jurisdictions on planet Earth.

And all at the precise moment when the U.S. is once again in
desperate need of a domestic source of supply.

I've prepared a full briefing explaining the situation and why
this story could become extremely important for investors.

Click here
( )
now to read the full report. Best,

Robert Ross
Editor, Let’s Analyze

P.S. When Roosevelt called on America to become the “Arsenal of
Democracy,” the nation mobilized its factories and its mines.

Today, the same strategic metal that helped power that effort is
suddenly back in the spotlight... and one little-known company in
Nevada may be sitting right at the center of the story.

So take my advice — don’t wait to start your due diligence on
this opportunity. Click here
( )
now to see my full analysis.

subject: Trump's tariffs, one year in
body:
Can you guess the impacts?

Global news you can trust.

Download the Reuters App.

Tariff Watch

Tariff Watch

Stay ahead of global tariff news with the latest headlines from Reuters .

Draw it yourself: Checking Trump’s tariff claims a year later

On April 2, 2025, a little over two months into his second term, US President Donald Trump unveiled sweeping tariffs on imports from almost all countries.

Since that pivotal moment in Trump's trade agenda, the tariffs on many impacted countries have been renegotiated, caveated and even revoked. In February, the Supreme Court ruled that Trump didn’t have the constitutional authority to impose universal tariffs. He quickly re-imposed a temporary global tariff of 10% on all US imports.

But, a year after "Liberation Day", what really has happened in the US economy? Have tariffs had the impacts Trump claimed they would?

We are giving you, the reader, the power to guess for yourself. Click here or on the headline above to access an interactive graphic. Draw on the charts what you think happened to the trade deficit, trade with China and tariff revenue, among other metrics, and then see how correctly you guessed.

Delta cuts growth amid fuel spike: Delta Air Lines ( DAL ) is reducing capacity growth as fuel costs jump $2 B, though Q1 earnings beat estimates and its refinery is expected to add a $300 M boost, highlighting cost pressures but operational resilience. Sources: CNBC / WSJ .

•
Airlines raise fees amid fuel surge: Delta Air Lines ( DAL ) and Southwest Airlines ( LUV ) are raising checked bag fees by $10 as jet fuel prices jump nearly 88%, passing the rising costs to consumers. Source: CNBC .

•
Ford petitions for tariff relief: Ford ( F ) is seeking tariff relief after plant outages forced reliance on higher-cost imported aluminum, but the Trump administration is holding firm, adding margin pressure and risking disruption to F-150 production. Source: WSJ .

•
Samsung block trade planned: A Samsung Electronics ( SSNLF ) shareholder is seeking to sell up to $2.1 B in shares via a discounted block trade, adding potential near-term stock pressure. Source: Bloomberg .

•
Blue Owl outlook cut to negative: Blue Owl Capital ( OWL ) faces a Moody’s outlook downgrade as heavy redemptions hit its $36 B credit fund, signaling broader stress in private credit with ~$13 B in investor withdrawals and slowing inflows. Source: Bloomberg .

•
Sony reshapes studio workforce: Sony Group’s ( SONY ) Sony Pictures Entertainment plans to cut hundreds of jobs across film, TV, and corporate units as it shifts toward franchises, anime, and games. Source: LA Times .

•
Levi Strauss boosts DTC growth: Levi Strauss ( LEVI ) reported 14% revenue growth and raised its guidance as direct-to-consumer sales exceed 50% of revenue, improving margins despite reliance on price increases and potential consumer slowdown risks. Source: CNBC .

•
U.S. automakers clash with EU: U.S. carmakers, including Ford ( F ) and GM ( GM ), are accusing the EU of blocking large pickup trucks via safety regulations, limiting growth in a key export market. Source: Reuters .

•
Shell flags gas output hit: Shell ( SHEL ) has cut its Q1 gas production outlook due to Qatar disruptions from the Iran conflict, while oil trading profits surge and working capital swings up to a $15 B outflow amid extreme price volatility. Source: Reuters .

Economic News

•
Oil falls on ceasefire deal: Oil prices fell, with WTI falling over 16% to below $95, after the U.S. and Iran agreed to a two-week ceasefire and partial reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. However, lasting geopolitical risks may keep market prices elevated. Sources: CNBC / Reuters .

•
Jet fuel recovery to lag oil: The International Air Transport Association warns that jet fuel supply may take months to normalize even if Hormuz reopens, as refinery disruptions will keep prices elevated. Source: Reuters .

•
Used car prices climb again: U.S. used vehicle prices rose 6.2% Yo Y to their highest level since mid-2023, as tight supply and new-car affordability pressures sustain demand, though full-year sales are still expected to decline slightly. Source: CNBC .

•
U.K. builders face cost surge: U.K. construction firms report record input cost inflation, with S&P Global PMI input prices hitting 70.5 and activity contracting for a 15th month. Source: Reuters .

AI News

Photo by Geoffrey Moffett on Unsplash

•
Anthropic expands AI security testing: Anthropic is giving Amazon ( AMZN ), Apple ( AAPL ), Microsoft ( MSFT ), and Cisco ( CSCO ) access to its unreleased Mythos model to identify cyber risks, aiming to strengthen safeguards before any broader launch. Source: Bloomberg .

•
Alibaba focuses on AI infrastructure: Alibaba Group ( BABA ) is launching a data center with 10,000 in-house Zhenwu AI chips, alongside China Telecom, boosting China’s push for domestic semiconductors and scaling AI model capabilities. Source: CNBC .

•
Tik Tok plans another data center: Tik Tok is planning a €1 B (USD $1.16 B) second data center in Finland to boost European data sovereignty and localize storage for over 200 M users. Source: Reuters .

•
Musk targets Open AI leadership: Elon Musk is seeking to remove Open AI CEO Sam Altman and President Greg Brockman in a lawsuit alleging fraud and nonprofit breaches, escalating a high-stakes AI rivalry ahead of an April trial. Source: CNBC .

World News & Politics

•
Fragile Middle East deal reached: The U.S. and Iran agreed to a two-week ceasefire tied to reopening the Strait of Hormuz, though disputes over terms and continued fighting in Lebanon highlight ongoing instability. Both sides claim the deal as a victory, though key issues — like Iran’s nuclear program — remain unresolved. Source: NPR .

•
Starmer backs ceasefire diplomacy: U.K. PM Keir Starmer is visiting Gulf states to support the fragile U.S.-Iran ceasefire and push to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, highlighting global efforts to stabilize energy flows and reduce economic risks. Source: BBC .

•
India resumes Iranian oil imports: India is set to receive its first Iranian crude cargo since 2019, with the Indian Oil Corporation ( NSE:IOC ) buying shipments after temporary U.S. sanctions relief. Source: Reuters .

•
Greece plans teen social media ban: Starting in 2027, Greece plans to ban social media use for children under 15 in order to address concerns over mental health, sleep disruption, and screen addiction. PM Kyriakos Mitsotakis is urging the EU to adopt a bloc-wide policy. Source: Reuters .

•
Democrats gain ground in elections: Recent elections in Georgia and Wisconsin showed significant gains for Democrats, including a 25-point shift in a Republican-held Georgia district and a decisive liberal victory in Wisconsin’s Supreme Court race. Source: NY Times .

•
Data center dispute turns violent: Shots were fired at an Indianapolis councilman’s home after he backed a data center re-zoning, underscoring escalating local opposition to rapid data center expansion. Source: NBC .
